title: Kubernetes
summary: A to-the-point review of the Kubernetes an SDET gets asked about: what the control plane does, Pods / Deployments / Services / Ingress, ConfigMaps & Secrets, health probes, scaling & self-healing, and how you deploy and test apps on a cluster. Builds on the Docker page — containers are the unit K8s schedules. Short explanations, short examples.

sections.title: What Kubernetes Is — and Why
sections.bodyHtml:
Docker runs a container on a host. Kubernetes (K8s) runs thousands across a fleet of machines — scheduling them, restarting failures, scaling on load, and rolling out new versions with zero downtime.

You declare the desired state (“3 replicas of this image”); Kubernetes continuously reconciles reality to match it.

  • Self-healing — a crashed Pod is recreated; a dead node’s Pods are rescheduled.
  • Horizontal scaling — add/remove replicas by hand or automatically on CPU/metrics.
  • Rolling updates & rollback — ship a new version gradually, revert instantly if it’s bad.
  • Service discovery & load balancing — stable names and traffic spreading across replicas.
Mental model: Docker is the package; Kubernetes is the operations team that runs the packages and keeps them alive.

sections.title: Cluster Architecture
sections.bodyHtml:
A cluster = a control plane (the brain) + worker nodes (where Pods run).

ComponentRole
API serverthe front door — every command/tool talks to it
etcdkey-value store holding the whole cluster state
schedulerdecides which node a new Pod runs on
controller managerreconciliation loops driving actual → desired state
kubeletagent on each node that runs & reports on Pods
kube-proxynode networking rules for Services
The core idea — control loops: controllers constantly compare desired state (your YAML) to actual state and act to close the gap. That loop is why K8s self-heals.

sections.title: Pods, ReplicaSets & Deployments
sections.bodyHtml:
• Pod — the smallest unit: one (or a few tightly-coupled) containers sharing a network/IP and storage. Pods are ephemeral — they come and go with new IPs.
  • ReplicaSet — keeps N identical Pods running.
  • Deployment — manages ReplicaSets to give you rolling updates and rollbacks; what you actually write for stateless apps.
  • StatefulSet — for stateful apps (databases) needing stable identity and storage; DaemonSet — one Pod per node (agents); Job/CronJob — run-to-completion / scheduled tasks.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata: { name: web }
spec:
  replicas: 3                 # desired state
  selector: { matchLabels: { app: web } }
  template:
    metadata: { labels: { app: web } }
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: myapp:1.2
          ports: [{ containerPort: 3000 }]
Say this: you rarely create Pods directly — you create a Deployment, and it manages the Pods (and their replacement on update or failure) for you.

sections.title: Services & Ingress
sections.bodyHtml:
Pods are ephemeral with changing IPs, so you never talk to a Pod directly. A Service gives a stable virtual IP + DNS name and load-balances across the matching Pods.

TypeReachable from
ClusterIP (default)inside the cluster only
NodePorta fixed port on every node
LoadBalanceran external cloud load balancer

Ingress sits in front of Services as an HTTP(S) router — host/path rules, TLS termination — so many apps share one external entry point instead of one LoadBalancer each.

Selector glue: a Service finds its Pods by label selector (app: web), not by IP — which is how it keeps working as Pods are replaced.

sections.title: ConfigMaps & Secrets
sections.bodyHtml:
• ConfigMap — non-sensitive config (URLs, feature flags) injected as env vars or mounted files. Keeps config out of the image.
  • Secret — same idea for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS keys); base64-encoded and access-controlled (enable encryption-at-rest / external secret stores in prod).
  • Namespaces — virtual partitions of one cluster (e.g. dev, staging, test) for isolation and quotas.
env:
  - name: LOG_LEVEL
    valueFrom: { configMapKeyRef: { name: app-config, key: log_level } }
  - name: DB_PASSWORD
    valueFrom: { secretKeyRef: { name: db-secret, key: password } }
Interview note: a Secret is only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default — call that out and mention RBAC, encryption-at-rest, and tools like Sealed Secrets / Vault.

sections.title: Health Probes & Self-Healing
sections.bodyHtml:
K8s keeps apps healthy using probes — periodic checks the kubelet runs against each container.

  • Liveness — “is it alive?” Fails → container is restarted. Catches deadlocks.
  • Readiness — “can it serve traffic?” Fails → Pod is pulled from the Service’s load balancer (but not killed). Catches warm-up/dependency waits.
  • Startup — guards slow-starting apps so liveness doesn’t kill them prematurely.
readinessProbe:
  httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 3000 }
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
Liveness vs readiness — the classic question: liveness failing restarts the container; readiness failing only removes it from rotation until it recovers. Mixing them up causes restart loops.

sections.title: Scaling & Rollouts
sections.bodyHtml:
• Manual scale — kubectl scale deploy/web --replicas=5.
  • HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) — adds/removes replicas automatically on CPU/memory/custom metrics.
  • Rolling update — the default: replace Pods gradually so there’s no downtime; kubectl rollout undo reverts.
  • Resource requests/limits — requests guide scheduling; limits cap usage (over the memory limit → the container is OOM-killed).
kubectl set image deploy/web web=myapp:1.3   # trigger a rolling update
kubectl rollout status deploy/web            # watch it
kubectl rollout undo  deploy/web             # roll back if bad

sections.title: Kubernetes for Testing & CI
sections.bodyHtml:
Where an SDET actually touches K8s:

  • Ephemeral test environments — spin up a namespace (or a whole throwaway cluster) per branch/PR, deploy the app, run E2E, tear it down.
  • Local clusters — kind (Kubernetes-in-Docker) or minikube to run a real cluster in CI or on your laptop.
  • kubectl for debugging — get, describe, logs, exec, port-forward are your triage toolkit when a test deployment misbehaves.
  • Helm — templated, versioned manifests (“charts”) so the same deploy is parameterized across dev/test/prod.
kubectl get pods -n test                 # what's running
kubectl describe pod web-xyz -n test     # events: why is it pending/crashing?
kubectl logs -f web-xyz -n test          # app output
kubectl port-forward svc/web 8080:80     # hit the service locally for a test
First triage move: a Pod stuck Pending or CrashLoopBackOff? kubectl describe pod — the Events section almost always names the cause (no node fits, image pull failed, failing probe).

quiz.question: Docker vs Kubernetes?
choices: Docker and Kubernetes both build images, but Kubernetes adds a registry layer
Docker runs a container on a host; Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across a cluster with scheduling, self-healing, scaling, and rollouts
Kubernetes builds containers while Docker only schedules them across nodes
Docker and Kubernetes are competing container runtimes that cannot coexist
quiz.correctAnswer: Docker runs a container on a host; Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across a cluster with scheduling, self-healing, scaling, and rollouts
quiz.explanation: Docker builds and runs a container on a host; Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across a cluster — scheduling, self-healing, scaling, and rollouts.

quiz.question: What is a Pod?
choices: A single container plus its image, treated as one immutable unit
A group of nodes that share networking and storage across the cluster
The smallest deployable unit: one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing a network/IP and storage, and is ephemeral
A permanent worker that keeps a fixed IP and persistent disk for its lifetime
quiz.correctAnswer: The smallest deployable unit: one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing a network/IP and storage, and is ephemeral
quiz.explanation: The smallest deployable unit: one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing a network/IP and storage. Pods are ephemeral.

quiz.question: Pod vs Deployment?
choices: A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet of identical Pods and gives you rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing; you create the Deployment, not Pods directly
A Pod manages Deployments, scaling them up and down based on load
A Deployment is a single Pod with extra metadata for versioning
Pods handle rollbacks while Deployments only define the container image
quiz.correctAnswer: A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet of identical Pods and gives you rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing; you create the Deployment, not Pods directly
quiz.explanation: A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet of identical Pods and gives you rolling updates, rollbacks, and self-healing — you create the Deployment, not Pods directly.

quiz.question: Why do you need a Service?
choices: A Service stores the Pod's persistent data so it survives restarts
Pods are ephemeral with changing IPs; a Service gives a stable IP/DNS name and load-balances across Pods matching its label selector
A Service schedules Pods onto nodes and restarts them when they crash
Services encrypt traffic between Pods using their fixed individual IPs
quiz.correctAnswer: Pods are ephemeral with changing IPs; a Service gives a stable IP/DNS name and load-balances across Pods matching its label selector
quiz.explanation: Pods are ephemeral with changing IPs; a Service provides a stable IP/DNS name and load-balances across the Pods matching its label selector.

quiz.question: ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer?
choices: ClusterIP exposes a node port; NodePort is internal; LoadBalancer routes HTTP paths
All three are external; they differ only in which cloud provider they target
ClusterIP = internal only; NodePort = a fixed port on each node; LoadBalancer = an external cloud LB; Ingress routes HTTP across Services
ClusterIP = external LB; NodePort = internal only; LoadBalancer = a port per node
quiz.correctAnswer: ClusterIP = internal only; NodePort = a fixed port on each node; LoadBalancer = an external cloud LB; Ingress routes HTTP across Services
quiz.explanation: ClusterIP = internal only; NodePort = a fixed port on each node; LoadBalancer = an external cloud LB. Ingress routes HTTP across many Services.

quiz.question: Liveness vs readiness probe?
choices: Liveness failing restarts the container; readiness failing removes the Pod from the Service load balancer until it recovers, with no restart
Liveness removes the Pod from rotation; readiness restarts the container on failure
Both probes restart the container, but liveness runs more frequently
Liveness checks dependencies; readiness checks for deadlocks and kills the node
quiz.correctAnswer: Liveness failing restarts the container; readiness failing removes the Pod from the Service load balancer until it recovers, with no restart
quiz.explanation: Liveness failing restarts the container; readiness failing removes the Pod from the Service load balancer until it recovers (no restart).

quiz.question: ConfigMap vs Secret?
choices: A Secret is fully encrypted at rest by default, while a ConfigMap is plaintext
ConfigMaps hold sensitive data and Secrets hold non-sensitive config
Both inject config; ConfigMap is for non-sensitive data, Secret for sensitive — but a Secret is only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default
Secrets are mounted as files only, while ConfigMaps can only be env vars
quiz.correctAnswer: Both inject config; ConfigMap is for non-sensitive data, Secret for sensitive — but a Secret is only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default
quiz.explanation: Both inject config as env/files; ConfigMap is for non-sensitive data, Secret for sensitive — but a Secret is only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default.

quiz.question: How does self-healing work?
choices: The API server pings each Pod and emails an operator when one dies
etcd stores backup Pods and swaps them in instantly when one fails
The scheduler restarts the entire node whenever any container exits
Controllers run reconciliation loops comparing actual to desired state and recreate/reschedule Pods to close the gap
quiz.correctAnswer: Controllers run reconciliation loops comparing actual to desired state and recreate/reschedule Pods to close the gap
quiz.explanation: Controllers run reconciliation loops comparing actual to desired state and recreate/reschedule Pods to close the gap.

quiz.question: How do you scale?
choices: kubectl scale, or an HPA that adjusts replicas automatically based on CPU/memory/custom metrics
Edit etcd directly to add more Pod entries to the cluster state
Add more containers to a single Pod until the load is handled
Restart the Deployment, which doubles the replica count each time
quiz.correctAnswer: kubectl scale, or an HPA that adjusts replicas automatically based on CPU/memory/custom metrics
quiz.explanation: kubectl scale, or an HPA that adjusts replicas automatically based on CPU/memory/custom metrics.

quiz.question: How do you do a zero-downtime deploy?
choices: Delete all old Pods, then create the new ones once they are gone
A rolling update replaces Pods gradually behind a readiness probe; kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous version
Scale to zero, swap the image, then scale back up to restore traffic
Take the Service offline, update every Pod at once, then bring it back
quiz.correctAnswer: A rolling update replaces Pods gradually behind a readiness probe; kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous version
quiz.explanation: A rolling update replaces Pods gradually behind a readiness probe; kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous version.

quiz.question: requests vs limits?
choices: limits reserve resources for scheduling; requests cap usage and trigger OOM kills
requests reserve resources for scheduling; limits cap them — exceeding the memory limit gets the container OOM-killed
requests and limits both cap usage, but requests apply only to CPU
requests cap usage while limits are advisory hints ignored by the scheduler
quiz.correctAnswer: requests reserve resources for scheduling; limits cap them — exceeding the memory limit gets the container OOM-killed
quiz.explanation: requests reserve resources for scheduling; limits cap them — exceeding the memory limit gets the container OOM-killed.

quiz.question: How do you debug a failing Pod?
choices: Restart the cluster and watch whether the Pod comes back healthy
Check etcd for the Pod's stored error log and stack trace
kubectl describe pod (read the Events), kubectl logs, and exec/port-forward — Pending/CrashLoopBackOff usually explains itself in the events
kubectl delete pod, since recreating it is the only way to see the failure cause
quiz.correctAnswer: kubectl describe pod (read the Events), kubectl logs, and exec/port-forward — Pending/CrashLoopBackOff usually explains itself in the events
quiz.explanation: kubectl describe pod (read the Events), kubectl logs, and exec/port-forward — Pending/CrashLoopBackOff usually explains itself in the events.
